title: Build Adjacency List
description: Given an undirected graph with n vertices and m edges, build its adjacency list and print neighbors of each vertex in increasing insertion order.
inputFormat: m lines each containing u v with 0-based vertices
outputFormat: For each vertex i from 0 to n-1, print: i: neighbors
intendedApproachSummary: Create a list of neighbor lists and add both directions for every undirected edge.
wrongApproachHints: Do not forget the reverse edge for undirected graphs.
Printing only edges is not the same as building adjacency lists.
hints: Initialize n empty lists.
For edge u-v, add v to u and u to v.

examples.explanation: Each vertex lists the nodes directly connected to it.

title: DFS Traversal from Node 0
description: Given an undirected graph, print the DFS traversal starting from node 0, visiting neighbors in the order they were provided.
outputFormat: Print the DFS traversal from node 0
intendedApproachSummary: Build adjacency lists and recursively or iteratively visit unvisited neighbors from node 0.
wrongApproachHints: Do not revisit nodes.
This problem asks only for the component reachable from node 0.
hints: Use a visited array.
Visit a node before recursing on neighbors.

examples.explanation: DFS dives down one branch before backtracking.

title: BFS Traversal from Node 0
description: Given an undirected graph, print the BFS traversal starting from node 0, visiting neighbors in the order they were provided.
outputFormat: Print the BFS traversal from node 0
intendedApproachSummary: Use a queue, push node 0, and process nodes breadth-first while marking visited nodes early.
wrongApproachHints: Mark nodes visited when enqueueing to avoid duplicates.
BFS is queue-based, not recursion-first.
hints: Offer node 0 to the queue first.
Poll front, then enqueue unvisited neighbors.

examples.explanation: BFS processes by layers of edge distance.

functionContract.javaSignature: public List<Integer> bfs(int n, List<List<Integer>> graph)
functionContract.cppSignature: vector<int> bfs(int n, const vector<vector<int>>& graph)

title: Count Connected Components
description: Given an undirected graph, print the number of connected components.
outputFormat: Print the number of connected components
intendedApproachSummary: Run DFS or BFS from every unvisited vertex and count how many launches are needed.
wrongApproachHints: Starting from node 0 only is not enough for disconnected graphs.
hints: Loop over every node.
Each fresh traversal marks one component.

title: Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph
description: Given an undirected graph, print Cycle if it contains a cycle, otherwise print Acyclic.
intendedApproachSummary: Run DFS/BFS with parent tracking. Seeing an already visited neighbor that is not the parent means a cycle exists.
wrongApproachHints: In an undirected graph, the parent edge should not count as a cycle.
hints: Pass parent into DFS.
If visited[nei] and nei != parent, a cycle exists.

title: Detect Cycle in Directed Graph
description: Given a directed graph, print Cycle if it contains a cycle, otherwise print Acyclic.
inputFormat: m lines of directed edges u v
intendedApproachSummary: Use Kahn's algorithm or DFS recursion-state tracking. If not all nodes can be processed topologically, a cycle exists.
wrongApproachHints: Undirected parent logic does not work here.
hints: Compute indegrees for Kahn's algorithm.
If processed count < n, the graph has a cycle.

title: Number of Islands
description: Given a binary grid of 0s and 1s, print the number of connected islands of 1s using 4-direction movement.
intendedApproachSummary: Treat each land cell as a graph node and run BFS/DFS to mark the whole island.
wrongApproachHints: Diagonal cells do not connect here unless stated.
Do not revisit cells.
hints: Use direction arrays.
Launch a traversal only from unvisited land.

title: Shortest Path in Binary Matrix
description: Given a grid where 0 means open and 1 means blocked, print the length of the shortest 4-direction path from top-left to bottom-right, or -1 if unreachable.
outputFormat: Print the shortest path length or -1
intendedApproachSummary: Use BFS from the start cell, because every move costs 1.
wrongApproachHints: DFS does not guarantee the shortest path in an unweighted grid.
hints: Store distance in the queue state or in a dist grid.
Mark visited when enqueueing.

examples.explanation: The shortest valid 4-direction path has length 5 nodes.

title: Topological Sort
description: Given a DAG, print one valid topological ordering.
intendedApproachSummary: Use Kahn's algorithm with indegrees and a queue of zero-indegree nodes.
wrongApproachHints: A normal BFS traversal order is not automatically a topological order.
hints: Compute indegree for every node.
Whenever indegree hits zero, enqueue that node.

examples.explanation: This is one valid topological ordering.

title: Course Schedule
description: Given numCourses and prerequisite pairs a b meaning b must be completed before a, print Possible if all courses can be finished, otherwise Impossible.
intendedApproachSummary: Use Kahn's algorithm and check whether all courses are processed.
wrongApproachHints: You do not need to output the order, just whether one exists.
hints: If processed count == n, finishing all courses is possible.

title: Shortest Path in Unweighted Graph
description: Given an unweighted undirected graph and a source node s, print the minimum distance from s to every vertex, using -1 for unreachable vertices.
intendedApproachSummary: BFS from the source computes minimum edge counts naturally in an unweighted graph.
wrongApproachHints: DFS can reach all nodes but not guarantee minimum distance.
hints: Initialize all distances to -1.
When visiting a neighbor first time, set dist[nei] = dist[node] + 1.

examples.explanation: Each value is the minimum number of edges from source 0.

title: Dijkstra Shortest Path
description: Given a weighted undirected graph with non-negative weights and a source s, print the shortest distance from s to every node, using -1 for unreachable vertices.
intendedApproachSummary: Use a min-priority queue of (distance, node) and relax edges whenever a shorter path is found.
wrongApproachHints: BFS only works for equal edge weights.
Skip stale heap entries when their stored distance is larger than the current best.
hints: Initialize dist[s] = 0.
When dist[node] + w improves dist[nei], push the new pair.

examples.explanation: The best path to 4 is 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 4 with total weight 6.

title: Network Delay Time
description: Given directed travel times and a source node k, print the minimum time for the signal to reach every node, or -1 if some node is unreachable.
inputFormat: m lines u v w with 1-based nodes
outputFormat: Print the network delay time or -1
intendedApproachSummary: Run Dijkstra from k and take the maximum shortest distance if all nodes are reachable.
wrongApproachHints: The answer is the slowest shortest path, not the sum of all shortest paths.
hints: After Dijkstra, if any distance is still infinite, answer is -1.
Otherwise print the maximum distance.

examples.explanation: The farthest node is reached in 2 time units.

title: Number of Provinces using DSU
description: Given an n x n adjacency matrix of an undirected graph, print the number of provinces using disjoint set union.
inputFormat: n lines each containing n integers 0 or 1
intendedApproachSummary: Union every connected pair, then count the number of distinct parents.
wrongApproachHints: Do not union the same relationship twice if you can avoid it.
Use path compression to keep operations fast.
hints: Only scan j > i in the matrix.
Count unique roots at the end.

examples.explanation: Cities 0 and 1 form one province, city 2 forms another.

title: Kruskal Minimum Spanning Tree
description: Given a connected weighted undirected graph, print the total weight of its minimum spanning tree using Kruskal's algorithm.
edgeCases: Multiple possible MST edges of same weight
intendedApproachSummary: Sort edges by weight and add an edge only if it connects two different DSU components.
wrongApproachHints: Do not add an edge that forms a cycle.
Sorting by edge weight is essential.
hints: Union the endpoints after taking an edge.
Stop after taking n - 1 edges.

examples.explanation: The MST uses edges of weights 4, 5, and 10.

title: Prim Minimum Spanning Tree
description: Given a connected weighted undirected graph, print the total weight of its minimum spanning tree using Prim's algorithm.
intendedApproachSummary: Use a min-priority queue of candidate edges, always taking the lightest edge that reaches a new vertex.
wrongApproachHints: Do not add weights for vertices already in the MST.
Prim grows one connected tree; it does not sort all edges first.
hints: Track visited vertices.
Push all outgoing edges of a newly added vertex.

examples.explanation: Prim reaches the same MST cost as Kruskal.
